返回本批次

隐形信号正在绕过伊朗的互联网封锁

Stealth signals are bypassing Iran’s internet blackout

spectrum.ieee.org 作者 WaitWaitWha 分数 80 评论 23 2026/04/16 10:16
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 2026 年 1 月 8 日，伊朗政府实施了几乎完全的通讯关闭。这是该国第一次全面信息封锁：数周以来，所有省份的互联网都处于关闭状态，而包括政府运营的内联网、VPN、短信、移动电话甚至固定电话在内的服务都受到严重限制。这是一场史无前例的封锁，导致超过 9000 万人不仅与世界隔绝，而且与彼此隔绝。从那时起，连通性就再也没有完全恢复过。继美国和以色列2月底发动空袭后，伊朗再次实施了近乎全面的限制，伊朗境内的人们再次看到全球信息流枯竭。一月份最初的封锁是在全国范围内因经济危机不断加深和政治镇压而引发的抗议活动中发生的，数百万人在街头高呼反政府口号。尽管近年来伊朗抗议活动频繁，但这是自1979年伊斯兰革命以来最重大的起义之一。政府反应迅速而残酷。一份报告称，死亡人数已确认超过 7,000 人，另有 11,000 多人正在接受调查。许多消息来源认为死亡人数可能超过 30,000 人。一月份关闭已经十三天了，我们网络自由先锋 (NFP) 转向了我们为这种时刻而构建的系统，即通过普通卫星电视信号发送文件的系统。在国家信息真空期间，我们名为 Toosheh 的技术向伊朗提供了实时更新，为数百万人缺乏可信信息提供了生命线。伊朗如何审查互联网 我于 2014 年加入了 NetFreedom Pioneers，这是一家专注于反审查技术的非营利组织。伊朗的审查制度是我 20 世纪 90 年代青年时期的一个决定性特征。伊斯兰革命后，大多数伊朗人开始过着双重生活——一种是在家里，他们可以喝酒、跳舞、选择自己的衣服；另一种是在公共场合，每个人都必须遵守令人窒息的政府法律。伊朗的互联网基础设施比世界其他地区更加集中，这使得政府更容易限制信息流动。 Morteza Nikoubazl/NurPhoto/Getty Images 我第一次接触秘密通信是在我五岁的时候，当时我住在伊朗南部的小城市法萨。我叔叔带回了一个卫星天线（当时是危险的非法行为），它可以让我们收听 12 个卫星频道。我最喜欢的是卡通频道。然后，在我十几岁的时候，这位叔叔通过拨号调制解调器向我介绍了互联网。我记得使用具有 4 MB 存储空间的雅虎邮箱，阅读世界各地的新闻，并从 NASA 的网站了解钱德拉 X 射线望远镜。这种开放性并没有持续多久。随着 2000 年代初互联网的普及，伊朗政府开始重塑网络本身。与美国或欧洲高度分布式的网络不同，伊朗与全球互联网的连接相对集中，其中数千家提供商通过许多独立的路线交换流量。大多数国际流量通过由国家相关电信运营商控制的少数网关。这种架构为当局提供了不同寻常的影响力：通过限制或撤回这些连接，他们可以大幅减少该国与外界的接触。在过去的十年中，伊朗通过所谓的国家信息网络扩大了这种控制，这是一个国内路由系统，旨在尽可能将数据保留在国内。许多政府服务、银行系统和本地平台都托管在这个内部网络上。在动乱时期，对全球互联网的访问可能会受到限制或切断，而国内网络的部分内容仍可继续运行。政府通过重定向或阻止网站来开始审查活动。随着互联网使用的增长，它采用了更复杂的方法。例如，伊朗电信公司使用一种称为深度数据包检测的技术来实时分析数据包的内容。这种方法使其能够识别和阻止特定类型的流量，例如 VPN 连接、消息应用程序、社交媒体平台和被禁止的网站。卫星传输的隐蔽性Toosheh 的通信解决方法建立在中东和北非国家采用卫星电视的历史基础上。到了 2000 年代初期，卫星天线在伊朗已经很普遍。如今，尽管官方禁止，但大多数伊朗家庭仍可以观看卫星电视。与 DirecTV 和 Dish Network 等订阅服务不同，“免费”卫星电视广播未加密，任何人只要拥有卫星电视和接收器就可以接收，无需订阅。由于信号是开放的，用户还可以捕获并存储其携带的数据，而不是简单地观看直播。精通技术的人们了解到他们可以使用数字视频广播(DVB) 卡是一种连接到计算机并调谐到卫星频率的硬件，可将个人计算机转变为卫星接收器。这样，他们就可以在本地观看和存储媒体，并从专用渠道下载数据。许多伊朗公民拥有免费卫星天线，就像德黑兰这座公寓楼上的卫星天线一样，因此可以下载 Toosheh 传输，在互联网中断期间为他们提供生命线。Morteza Nikoubazl/NurPhoto/Getty ImagesToosheh，一个波斯语单词，翻译为“背包”，是伊朗裔美国技术专家和企业家 Mehdi Yahyanejad 的创意。 Yahyanejad 于 2012 年与他人共同创立了 NetFreedom Pioneers。他提出可以在软件中重新创建由 DVB 卡实现的卫星与计算机连接，从而无需专门的硬件。他在软件中添加了一个简单的数字界面，使任何人都可以轻松使用。当 NFP 团队开发了一种新的传输协议时，下一个突破出现了，该协议可以诱骗普通卫星接收器下载数据以及音频和视频内容。因此，Toosheh 诞生了。卫星电视使用一种称为 MPEG 传输流的文件系统，允许将多个音频、视频或数据层打包到单个流文件中。当您收听卫星频道并选择音频选项或隐藏式字幕时，您正在访问存储在该流的不同部分中的数据。 NFP 团队的见解是，通过利用这些层之一，Tooshih 可以发送包含文档、视频等的 MPEG 流。 TOOSHEH 的工作原理：在 NetFreedom Pioneers，内容管理者将文件整理在一起——新闻文章、视频、音频和软件 [1]。 Toosheh 的编码器软件 [2] 将文件压缩为 .ts 格式的捆绑包，创建 MPEG 传输流 [3]。从那里，它被上传到服务器，通过位于中东上空的 Yahsat 卫星上的免费电视频道进行传输 [4]，以提供区域覆盖 [5]。卫星接收器 [6] 直接捕获数据流，这些数据流被下载到计算机、智能手机和其他设备，并由 Toosheh 软件解码 [8]。 Chris Philpot 卫星接收器无法区分我们的数据和普通卫星音频和视频数据，因为它只“看到”MPEG 流，而不是其中编码的内容。这意味着数据可以下载并读取、观看并保存在计算机、智能手机或存储设备等本地设备上。更重要的是，该系统是完全私密的：没有人可以检测到某人是否通过Toosheh接收过数据；没有可追踪的用户活动日志。Toosheh 不提供互联网访问，而是通过卫星技术提供精选数据。根本区别在于用户与系统交互的方式。与传统的互联网服务（在浏览器中输入请求并接收响应数据）不同，Toosheh 的运作方式更像是广播和电视的结合，以类似杂志的格式呈现信息。用户不提出请求；相反，他们会收到 1 到 5 GB 的预先打包、精心挑选的数据。获取信息不仅涉及新闻或政治，还涉及接触各种可能性。在今年的互联网封锁期间，我们分发了伊朗反对派领导人王储礼萨·巴列维和美国政府的官方声明。我们为医务人员和受伤的抗议者提供急救教程。我们发送了来自 BBC 波斯语、伊朗国际、IranWire、VOA 波斯语等未经审查的新闻报道。我们还分享了包括反审查和反监视工具在内的关键软件包，以及帮助人们安全连接到星链卫星终端的操作指南，使他们在发送自己的通信时保持受到保护和匿名。如何对抗信号干扰由于 Toosheh 依赖单向卫星广播，因此它规避了政府阻止互联网访问的常用策略。然而，它仍然容易受到卫星信号干扰。伊朗政府因部署信号干扰而臭名昭著，尤其是在大城市。 2009年，政府使用上行链路干扰，通过以卫星接收器频率发射强噪声来攻击在轨卫星。这使得卫星无法区分它应该接收的信息。然而，由于此类攻击暂时使整颗卫星瘫痪，伊朗受到国际制裁的威胁，并于 2012 年停止使用该方法。伊朗的网络连接图显示，2026 年 1 月 9 日，互联网访问从近 100% 下降到 0。Samuel Boivin/NurPhoto/Getty Images 目前的方法称为地面干扰，使用安装在比周围建筑物更高的高度的天线，在家用接收器频率范围内的特定区域发射强噪声。这次攻击是可以有效地阻止某些数据包到达并损坏其他数据包，从而有效地干扰传输。但它的范围短，需要很大的力量，因此不可能在全国范围内实施。尽管有干扰，总有人在某个地方仍然可以看电视、从 Toosheh 下载或收听卫星广播。即便如此，我们还是想要一种解决方法，使我们的传输能够广泛访问。NFP 的解决方案是添加冗余，原则上类似于称为 RAID（独立磁盘冗余阵列）的数据存储技术。我们不是发送每条数据一次，而是发送额外的信息，允许重建丢失或损坏的数据包。在正常情况下，我们经常使用 5% 的带宽来实现这种冗余。在主动干扰期间，我们将其提高到 25% 至 30%，从而提高了用户在受到干扰的情况下恢复完整文件的机会。从危机响应到公共访问Toosheh 最初于 2015 年在伊朗和阿富汗上线。然而，它的全部潜力在 2019 年伊朗的抗议活动中首次得到实现，今年停电之前，伊朗出现了最广泛的互联网关闭。 《连线》称 2019 年的关闭是 NetBlocks 追踪到的所有国家中“最严重的断网”，就其“技术复杂性和广度”而言。我们的技术帮助成千上万的人了解情况。我们向全国各地的卫星接收器发送了重要的本地更新、法律援助指南、数字安全工具和独立新闻，我们的用户群增加了六倍。当抗议浪潮平息后，政府允许一些通信服务恢复。人们再次能够使用 VPN 和其他允许他们绕过限制的反过滤软件访问免费互联网。随后，Toosheh 成为超越政府过滤的新闻、教育材料和娱乐的公共访问点。Toosheh 的影响往往是个人的。伊朗西部的一名旅行教师告诉 NFP，他定期向偏远村庄的学生分发 Toosheh 文件。其中一个包裹包含女运动员参加奥运会的镜头，而这些镜头从未在伊朗播放过。对于一个年轻女孩来说，这是她第一次意识到女性可以参加职业体育比赛。那一刻强调了一个更广泛的事实：获取信息不仅涉及新闻或政治，还涉及接触可能性。Toosheh 的成本与基于互联网的系统不同，无论用户数量有多少，Toosheh 的运营成本都保持不变。由欧洲通信卫星公司等国际公司部署和维护的地球静止轨道上的一颗电视卫星可以向整个大陆广播，而不会增加观众的成本。更重要的是，用户的启动成本并不高：在伊朗，一个卫星天线和接收器的成本不到 50 美元，很多人都可以承受。人们使用 Toosheh 的服务并接收其文件是免费的。我们的目标不仅仅是构建一个规避审查的工具，而是重新定义访问本身。然而，运营该服务的成本高昂：NetFreedom Pioneers 每月为卫星带宽支付数万美元。我们曾获得美国国务院的资助，但在 2025 年 8 月，资助结束，迫使我们暂停在伊朗的服务。然后 12 月的抗议活动发生，向伊朗广播成为当务之急。为了重新启用 Toosheh，我们每月大约需要 50,000 美元。在少数私人捐助者的支持下，我们能够支付这些费用并维持在伊朗的运营几个月，尽管我们在伊朗和其他地方的未来还不确定。Toosheh 卫星在伊朗的复兴伴随着 NFP 持续支持星链的部署，星链是一种卫星互联网服务，允许用户直接连接到卫星，而不是依赖国内网络，政府可以关闭国内网络。与 Toosheh 的单向广播不同，Starlink 提供完整的双向互联网接入，使用户能够发送消息、上传视频以及与外界沟通。2022 年，我们开始筹集捐款为伊朗购买 Starlink 终端。我们已经向那里发送了大约 50,000 个中的 300 多个，使公民能够从国内向我们发送加密的更新和视频。由于该技术被政府禁止，因此访问仍然受到限制并存在风险；伊朗当局最近逮捕了星链用户和卖家。与 Toosheh 的仅接收广播不同，星链终端将信号传输回轨道，从而形成可能被检测到的无线电足迹。自 2 月下旬以色列和美国开始发动袭击后，伊朗的互联网继续关闭，导致伊朗人无法与外界以及彼此之间进行沟通。Fatemeh Bahrami/Anadolu/Getty Images 展望未来，我们预计 Toosheh 会成为一个基础性的网络服务提供商。全球数字弹性的一部分。它未经审查、无法追踪，并且能够抵抗政府关闭。由于 Toosheh 仅提供下行链路，因此有时很难向那些生活在自由世界、习惯于开放互联网接入的人们解释这项技术的价值。然而，当数字中断时，生活在审查制度下的人们几乎没有其他选择。目前，NFP 正在开发新功能，例如智能内容管理以及根据地理或情况需求自动确定数据包的优先级。我们正在试验本地共享工具，允许接收 Toosheh 广播的用户通过 Wi-Fi 热点或其他离线网络重新分发这些文件，这可以将系统的覆盖范围扩展到基础设施可能被破坏的灾区、冲突地区和受气候影响的地区。我们还在研究其他用例。塔利班在阿富汗重新掌权后，网络自由先锋设计了一个基于卫星的系统来提供教育材料。我们的目标是向任何人（包括被禁止进入阿富汗学校的女孩）提供大规模的私人课程作业分发。该系统在技术上已经准备就绪，但尚未获得部署资金。我们的目标不仅仅是构建一个规避审查的工具，而是重新定义访问本身。无论是在受到监视的伊朗城市、没有互联网的危地马拉村庄，还是东非的难民营，Toosheh 都提供了一个强大而实用的模型，可以在不依赖脆弱或昂贵网络的情况下传递重要信息。Toosheh 提醒我们，创新并不一定意味着复杂。有时，最具变革性的想法是最简单的，比如通过天空，安静且经济地将数据传递到最需要的人手中。

Original Summary

  1. On 8 January 2026, the Iranian government imposed a near-total communications shutdown. It was the country’s first full information blackout: For weeks, the internet was off across all provinces while services including the government-run intranet, VPNs, text messaging, mobile calls, and even landlines were severely throttled. It was an unprecedented lockdown that left more than 90 million people cut off not only from the world, but from one another.Since then, connectivity has never fully returned. Following U.S. and Israeli airstrikes in late February, Iran again imposed near-total restrictions, and people inside the country again saw global information flows dry up.The original January shutdown came amid nationwide protests over the deepening economic crisis and political repression, in which millions of people chanted antigovernment slogans in the streets. While Iranian protests have become frequent in recent years, this was one of the most significant uprisings since the Islamic Revolution in 1979. The government responded quickly and brutally. One report put the death toll at more than 7,000 confirmed deaths and more than 11,000 under investigation. Many sources believe the death toll could exceed 30,000.Thirteen days into the January shutdown, we at NetFreedom Pioneers (NFP) turned to a system we had built for exactly this kind of moment—one that sends files over ordinary satellite TV signals. During the national information vacuum, our technology, called Toosheh, delivered real-time updates into Iran, offering a lifeline to millions starved of trusted information.How Iran Censors the InternetI joined NetFreedom Pioneers, a nonprofit focused on anticensorship technology, in 2014. Censorship in Iran was a defining feature of my youth in the 1990s. After the Islamic Revolution, most Iranians began to lead double lives—one at home, where they could drink, dance, and choose their clothing, and another in public, where everyone had to comply with stifling government laws. Iran’s internet infrastructure is more centralized than in other parts of the world, making it easier for the government to restrict the flow of information. Morteza Nikoubazl/NurPhoto/Getty ImagesMy first experience with secret communications was when I was five and living in the small city of Fasa in southern Iran. My uncle brought home a satellite dish—dangerously illegal at the time—that allowed us to tune into 12 satellite channels. My favorite was Cartoon Network. Then, during my teenage years, this same uncle introduced me to the internet through dial-up modems. I remember using Yahoo Mail with its 4 megabytes of storage, reading news from around the world, and learning about the Chandra X-ray telescope from NASA’s website. That openness didn’t last. As internet use spread in the early 2000s, the Iranian government began reshaping the network itself. Unlike the highly distributed networks in the United States or Europe, where thousands of providers exchange traffic across many independent routes, Iran’s connection to the global internet is relatively centralized. Most international traffic passes through a small number of gateways controlled by state-linked telecom operators. That architecture gives authorities unusual leverage: By restricting or withdrawing those connections, they can sharply reduce the country’s access to the outside world.Over the past decade, Iran has expanded this control through what it calls the National Information Network, a domestically routed system designed to keep data inside the country whenever possible. Many government services, banking systems, and local platforms are hosted on this internal network. During periods of unrest, access to the global internet can be throttled or cut off while portions of this domestic network continue to function.The government began its censorship campaign by redirecting or blocking websites. As internet use grew, it adopted more sophisticated approaches. For example, the Telecommunication Company of Iran uses a technique called deep packet inspection to analyze the content of data packets in real time. This method enables it to identify and block specific types of traffic, such as VPN connections, messaging apps, social media platforms, and banned websites.The Stealth of Satellite TransmissionsToosheh’s communication workaround builds on a history of satellite TV adoption in Middle Eastern and North African countries. By the early 2000s, satellite dishes were common in Iran; today the majority of households in Iran have access to satellite TV despite its official prohibition.Unlike subscription services such as DirecTV and Dish Network, “free-to-air” satellite TV broadcasts are unencrypted and can be received by anyone with a dish and receiver—no subscription required. Because the signals are open, users can also capture and store the data they carry, rather than simply watching it live. Tech-savvy people learned that they could use a digital video broadcasting (DVB) card—a piece of hardware that connects to a computer and tunes into satellite frequencies—to transform a personal computer into a satellite receiver. This way, they could watch and store media locally as well as download data from dedicated channels. Many Iranian citizens have free-to-air satellite dishes, like the ones on this apartment building in Tehran, and can thus download Toosheh transmissions, giving them a lifeline during internet blackouts.Morteza Nikoubazl/NurPhoto/Getty ImagesToosheh, a Persian word that translates to “knapsack,” is the brainchild of Mehdi Yahyanejad, an Iranian-American technologist and entrepreneur. Yahyanejad cofounded NetFreedom Pioneers in 2012. He proposed that the satellite-computer connections enabled by a DVB card could be re-created in software, eliminating the need for specialized hardware. He added a simple digital interface to the software to make it easy for anyone to use. The next breakthrough came when the NFP team developed a new transfer protocol that tricks ordinary satellite receivers into downloading data alongside audio and video content. Thus, Toosheh was born.Satellite TV uses a file system called an MPEG transport stream that allows multiple audio, video, or data layers to be packaged into a single stream file. When you tune in to a satellite channel and select an audio option or closed captions, you’re accessing data stored in different parts of this stream. The NFP team’s insight was that, by piggybacking on one of these layers, Toosheh could send an MPEG stream that included documents, videos, and more. HOW TOOSHEH WORKS: At NetFreedom Pioneers, content curators pull together files—news articles, videos, audio, and software [1]. Toosheh’s encoder software [2] compresses the files into a bundle, in .ts format, creating an MPEG transport stream [3]. From there, it’s uploaded to a server for transmission [4] via a free-to-air TV channel on a Yahsat satellite that’s positioned over the Middle East to provide regional coverage [5]. Satellite receivers [6] directly capture the data streams, which are downloaded to computers, smartphones, and other devices, and decoded by Toosheh software [8].Chris PhilpotA satellite receiver can’t tell the difference between our data and normal satellite audio and video data since it only “sees” the MPEG streams, not what’s encoded on them. This means the data can be downloaded and read, watched, and saved on local devices such as computers, smartphones, or storage devices. What’s more, the system is entirely private: No one can detect whether someone has received data through Toosheh; there are no traceable logs of user activity.Toosheh doesn’t provide internet access, but rather delivers curated data through satellite technology. The fundamental distinction lies in the way users interact with the system. Unlike traditional internet services, where you type a request into your browser and receive data in response, Toosheh operates more like a combination of radio and television, presenting information in a magazine-like format. Users don’t make requests; instead, they receive 1 to 5 gigabytes of prepackaged, carefully selected data.Access to information is not only about news or politics, but about exposure to possibilities. During this year’s internet blackout, we distributed official statements from Iranian opposition leader Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi and the U.S. government. We provided first-aid tutorials for medics and injured protesters. We sent uncensored news reports from BBC Persian, Iran International, IranWire, VOA Farsi, and others. We also shared critical software packages including anticensorship and antisurveillance tools, along with how-to guides to help people securely connect to Starlink satellite terminals, allowing them to stay protected and anonymous as they sent their own communications.How to Combat Signal InterferenceBecause Toosheh relies on one-way satellite broadcasts, it evades the usual tactics governments use to block internet access. However, it remains vulnerable to satellite signal jamming.The Iranian government is notorious for deploying signal jamming, especially in larger cities. In 2009, the government used uplink interference, which attacks the satellite in orbit by beaming strong noise in the frequency of the satellite’s receiver. This makes it impossible for the satellite to distinguish the information it’s supposed to receive. However, because this type of attack temporarily disables the entire satellite, Iran was threatened with international sanctions and in 2012 stopped using the method . A graph of network connectivity in Iran shows that on 9 January 2026, internet access dropped from nearly 100 percent to 0. Samuel Boivin/NurPhoto/Getty ImagesThe current method, called terrestrial jamming, uses antennas installed at higher elevations than the surrounding buildings to beam strong noise over a specific area in the frequency range of household receivers. This attack is effective in keeping some of the packets from arriving and damaging others, effectively jamming the transmission. But it’s short-range and requires significant power, so it’s impossible to implement nationwide. There are always people somewhere who can still watch TV, download from Toosheh, or tune into a satellite radio despite the jamming. Even so, we wanted a workaround that would keep our transmissions broadly accessible.NFP’s solution was to add redundancy, similar in principle to a data-storage technique called RAID (redundant array of independent disks). Instead of sending each piece of data once, we send extra information that allows missing or corrupted packets to be reconstructed. Under normal circumstances, we often use 5 percent of our bandwidth for this redundancy. During periods of active jamming, we increase that to as much as 25 to 30 percent, improving the chances that users can recover complete files despite interference.From Crisis Response to Public AccessToosheh initially came online in 2015 in Iran and Afghanistan. Its full potential, however, was first realized during the 2019 protests in Iran, which saw the most widespread internet shutdown prior to the blackout this year. Wired called the 2019 shutdown “the most severe disconnection” tracked by NetBlocks in any country in terms of its “technical complexity and breadth.” Our technology helped thousands of people stay informed. We sent crucial local updates, legal-aid guides, digital security tools, and independent news to satellite receivers all over the country, seeing a sixfold increase in our user base.When that wave of protests subsided, the government allowed some communication services to return. People were again able to access the free internet using VPNs and other antifilter software that allowed them to bypass restrictions. Toosheh then became a public access point for news, educational material, and entertainment beyond government filtering.Toosheh’s impact is often personal. A traveling teacher in western Iran told NFP that he regularly distributed Toosheh files to students in remote villages. One package included footage of female athletes competing in the Olympic Games, something never broadcast in Iran. For one young girl, it was the first time she realized women could compete professionally in sports. That moment underscores a broader truth: Access to information is not only about news or politics, but about exposure to possibilities.The Cost of TooshehUnlike internet-based systems, Toosheh’s operational cost remains constant regardless of the number of users. A single TV satellite in geostationary earth orbit, deployed and maintained by an international company such as Eutelsat, can broadcast to an entire continent with no increase in cost to audiences. What’s more, the startup cost for users isn’t high: A satellite dish and receiver in Iran costs less than US $50, which is affordable to many. And it costs nothing for people to use Toosheh’s service and receive its files.We aim not just to build a tool for censorship circumvention, but to redefine access itself. However, operating the service is costly: NetFreedom Pioneers pays tens of thousands of dollars a month for satellite bandwidth. We had received funding from the U.S. State Department, but in August of 2025, that funding ended, forcing us to suspend services in Iran.Then the December protests happened, and broadcasting to Iran became an urgent priority. To turn Toosheh back on, we needed roughly $50,000 a month. With the support of a handful of private donors, we were able to meet these costs and sustain operations in Iran for a few months, though our future there and elsewhere is uncertain.Satellites Against CensorshipToosheh’s revival in Iran came alongside NFP’s ongoing support for deployments of Starlink, a satellite internet service that allows users to connect directly to satellites rather than relying on domestic networks, which the government can shut down. Unlike Toosheh’s one-way broadcasts, Starlink provides full two-way internet access, enabling users to send messages, upload videos, and communicate with the outside world.In 2022, we started gathering donations to buy Starlink terminals for Iran. We have delivered more than 300 of the roughly 50,000 there, enabling citizens to send encrypted updates and videos to us from inside the country. Because the technology is banned by the government, access remains limited and carries risk; Iranian authorities have recently arrested Starlink users and sellers. And unlike Toosheh’s receive-only broadcasts, Starlink terminals transmit signals back to orbit, creating a radio footprint that can potentially be detected. The internet shutdown in Iran continued after the attacks by Israel and the United States began in late February, preventing Iranians from communicating with the outside world and with one another.Fatemeh Bahrami/Anadolu/Getty ImagesLooking ahead, we envision Toosheh becoming a foundational part of global digital resilience. It is uncensored, untraceable, and resistant to government shutdowns. Because Toosheh is downlink only, it can sometimes feel hard to explain the value of this technology to those living in the free world, those accustomed to open internet access. Yet, people living under censorship have few other choices when there’s a digital blackout.Currently, NFP is developing new features like intelligent content curation and automatically prioritizing data packages based on geographic or situational needs. And we’re experimenting with local sharing tools that allow users who receive Toosheh broadcasts to redistribute those files via Wi-Fi hotspots or other offline networks, which could extend the system’s reach to disaster zones, conflict areas, and climate-impacted regions where infrastructure may be destroyed.We’re also looking at other use cases. Following the Taliban’s return to power in Afghanistan, NetFreedom Pioneers designed a satellite-based system to deliver educational materials. Our goal is to enable private, large-scale distribution of coursework to anyone—including the girls who are banned from Afghanistan’s schools. The system is technically ready but has yet to secure funding for deployment.We aim not just to build a tool for censorship circumvention, but to redefine access itself. Whether in an Iranian city under surveillance, a Guatemalan village without internet, or a refugee camp in East Africa, Toosheh offers a powerful and practical model for delivering vital information without relying on vulnerable or expensive networks.Toosheh is a reminder that innovation doesn’t have to mean complexity. Sometimes, the most transformative ideas are the simplest, like delivering data through the sky, quietly and affordably, into the hands of those who need it most.

评论（默认中文）

thakoppno 2026/04/16 13:22
> 卫星电视使用称为 MPEG 传输流的文件系统，允许将多个音频、视频或数据层打包到单个流文件中。

读起来很有趣，但那部分确实让我质疑自己的理智。它可能只是在翻译中丢失了。

> Satellite TV uses a file system called an MPEG transport stream that allows multiple audio, video, or data layers to be packaged into a single stream file.

Interesting read but that part really made me question my own sanity. It’s probably just lost in translation.

在 HN 中查看
wmf 2026/04/16 13:36
我不会将传输流描述为“文件系统”，但大多数人可能不熟悉术语多路复用。确实，如果您录制 TS（例如录制到 .ts 文件），您可以稍后分割出不同的节目流，这些节目流可以容纳几乎任何内容。
I wouldn't describe Transport Stream as a "file system" but most people probably aren't familiar with the term multiplexing. It's true if you record a TS (e.g. to a .ts file) you can later split out the different Program Streams which can hold pretty much anything.
在 HN 中查看
djkoolaide 2026/04/16 13:30
这就是它的工作原理。将免费广播天线指向天空中的正确位置，解调 DVB-S2 信号，通常是通过 mpeg 传输流传输的 IP 流量。
that's how it works. point a free-to-air dish at the right place in the sky, demodulate the DVB-S2 signal, and it's often IP traffic moving through mpeg transport streams.
在 HN 中查看
thakoppno 2026/04/16 13:38
它是一个文件系统的说法让我很困惑。显然nbd，但也许我的思维模式又被打破了。
it’s the claim of it being a file system that’s throwing me. obviously nbd but maybe my mental models broken again.
在 HN 中查看
pabs3 2026/04/16 13:42
http://tom7.org/harder/
http://tom7.org/harder/
在 HN 中查看
bawolff 2026/04/16 11:59
那么它只是将一些文件嵌入卫星电视广播流中？

我认为这没有多大帮助。如果您有卫星电视，您已经有视频，那么任意文件真的那么有用吗？

人们真正想要的互联网通信是双向通信。获取有关当地局势的信息，并允许伊朗境内的不同团体进行协调。我不明白这有什么帮助。

So its just embeding some files in a satalite tv broadcast stream?

I dont think that helps that much. If you have satalite tv going in, you already have video coming in, are arbitrary files really that much more useful?

The thing people really want internet communication for is 2 way coms. Getting info on the situation on the ground out and allowing different groups inside Iran to coordinate. I fail to see how this helps with that.

在 HN 中查看
labelbabyjunior 2026/04/16 12:12
> 那么它只是将一些文件嵌入卫星电视广播流中？

这既不新鲜也不新颖。

46 年前，PlayCable 就这样做是为了通过模拟有线系统下载主机游戏<检查笔记>。

图文电视打招呼。

> So its just embeding some files in a satalite tv broadcast stream?

Which is neither new nor novel.

PlayCable was doing this to download console games over analog cable systems <checks notes> 46 years ago.

TeleText says hello.

在 HN 中查看
tgma 2026/04/16 11:51
我在伊朗认识的人中没有人真正使用过这个 Toosheh[1] 东西。我的意思是零，虚无，没有。没有一个。大多数人不知道它的存在。这听起来在星链时代之前听起来很酷，它获得了西方政府和非政府组织的资金和支持，但却没有产生任何有用的结果（毫不奇怪，尽管完全失败，但他们也得到了国际媒体的关注）。实际上，仅下载的解决方案并不能解决问题。人们观看的波斯视频内容是通过 DVB 卫星电视视频频道传送的。通过互联网，人们想要的是交流，因此需要实时访问和数据上传能力来联系他人和使用网络服务，而不是每天下载新的离线维基百科副本！实际上，伊朗境内的伊朗人最终主要使用各种 VPN 和隧道。通常，Shadowsock 的某些变体带有 SNI 欺骗功能，在完全停电时会停止工作。在全面停电期间，剩下的将是拥有政府批准的可完全上网的SIM卡（称为“白色SIM卡”）的人，他们可以在社交网络上宣传支持政权，而其他人都断网了，还有一小部分人购买了星链终端。

据称，该项目背后的同一群人获得了额外的资源来将星链走私进去，而在 Twitter 上的波斯社区中，有一个持续的表情包，嘲笑这些星链实际上去了哪里，给了谁，却永远得不到答案……

[1]：TLDR：DVB-S 视频流中的数据存档

None of the people I know inside Iran actually use this Toosheh[1] thing. And I mean zero, nada, none. Not one. Most are unaware of its existence. This sounds something that sounded cool pre-Starlink era that received funds and favors from western governments and NGOs and did not result in anything useful (not surprising that they get international press too despite being a total failure.) Realistically, a download-only solution does not solve a problem. Persian video content that people watch are delivered via DVB Satellite TV video channels. With Internet, what people want is to communicate and therefore need realtime access and data upload capability to contact others and use web services, not download a new offline copy of Wikipedia everyday! In practice, Iranians inside Iran end up mostly using VPNs and tunnels of various sorts. Often some variant of shadowsocks with SNI spoofing, which stop working in a full blackout. What will be left during a full blackout is people who have government-sanctioned SIM cards with full Internet access (known as "white SIMs") to propagandize on social networks in favor of the reigme when everyone else is disconnected and, a tiny set of people who have acquired Starlink terminals.

The same set of people behind that project were supposedly given additional resources to smuggle Starlinks inside, and in the Persian community on Twitter, there's an ongoing meme mocking where those Starlinks actually went and given to whom, never to get an answer...

[1]: TLDR: data archive within DVB-S video stream

在 HN 中查看
stackghost 2026/04/16 12:41
>在全面停电期间，剩下的将是那些拥有政府批准的可完全上网的SIM卡（称为“白色SIM卡”）的人，他们可以在社交网络上进行宣传，以支持当其他人都断网时的政权，以及一小部分拥有星链的人。

人们可能会认为这正是存储转发/延迟容忍路由有用的情况。 在 Jack Dorsey 想到 bitchat[0] 的几年前，我也有同样的想法，但从未追求过它，因为我生活在西方国家，而不是“科技城市”，换句话说，这里没有人有兴趣成为主要供末日准备者或生活在专制独裁政权下的人们使用的应用程序的早期采用者。

无论如何，这是一个好主意 理论上，正如技术无政府主义者准备的那样，LARP 和现成的 lilygo LoRa 收发器会很乐意告诉您。 但实际上，真正能从中受益的人似乎都没有采用这些东西。 或者至少我从来没有听说过，如果他们确实听说过的话。 也许今天的互联网中断对于 2026 年版本的地下出版物来说太短暂了，无法开发？

你在伊朗认识的人是否打算等待，或者他们是否有其他解决方案来应对全面停电？

[0] https://bitchat.free/

>What will be left during a full blackout is people who have government-sanctioned SIM cards with full Internet access (known as "white SIMs") to propagandize on social networks in favor of the reigme when everyone else is disconnected and a tiny set of people who have Starlink.

One would think this is exactly the sort of circumstances under which store-and-forward/delay-tolerant routing would be useful. Years before Jack Dorsey thought of bitchat[0] I had the same idea, but never pursued it because I live in a western country but not in a "tech city", in other words, nobody around here is interested in being an early adopter of an app primarily of use only to preppers or people living under repressive authoritarian regimes.

Anyways, it's a great idea in theory, as the techno-anarchist preppers that LARP with off-the-shelf lilygo LoRa tranceivers will be happy to tell you. But in practice nobody who actually could benefit from these seems to adopt these things. Or at least I never hear about it, if they indeed do. Perhaps today's internet blackouts are too transient for a 2026 version of samizdat to develop?

Do the people you know inside Iran plan to just wait it out, or do they have some other solution ready for a total blackout?

[0] https://bitchat.free/

在 HN 中查看
tgma 2026/04/16 12:47
需要明确的是，虽然烦人的防火墙一直是一件事情，甚至连奶奶都知道如何每天使用 VPN 来访问 Instagram，但全面、长期的封锁却是一个相对较新的事情，所以我认为对此没有足够的准备。一月份的抗议活动之后、战争爆发之前，Bitchat 无疑是人们谈论的话题。甚至有一个小偷克隆了它，并将其重新命名为波斯语，没有归属，安全性也很可疑，就在几周前，Bitchat 的家伙对此感到不安。

当然，有一些政府批准的信使显然仍在继续工作，但有些人不会使用它，因为他们完全不安全并且受到老大哥的监视。最大的问题是从国内获取数据而不是内部通信（例如，大屠杀的视频证据，这样像这个线程中的一些人就不会有意或无意地获得粉饰政权的弹药。）

To be clear while the annoying firewall has been a forever thing, and even grandmas know how to use VPNs day-to-day to access Instagram, a full, long-term blackout, has been a relatively new thing, so I don't think there's enough prep for that. Bitchat was certainly something that was spoken about after the January protests and before the war broke out. There was even a thief who cloned and renamed it something Persian without attribution and with shady security and the Bitchat guy got upset about it just a few weeks ago.

There are some government-sanctioned messengers that apparently keep working but some people would not use it as they are completely insecure and watched by big brother, of course. The biggest issue is getting data out of the country not internal comms (e.g. video evidence of massacre, for example, so that some poeple like in this very thread don't get the ammo to whitewash the regime, intentionally or accidentally.)

在 HN 中查看
stackghost 2026/04/16 13:24
>最大的问题是从国外获取数据而不是内部通信

无疑。 除非在阿塞拜疆或巴士拉之类的边境对面有友好的人，否则我不知道他们会怎么做。 也许点一道菜并建立点对点链接，但您需要预先安排。

>The biggest issue is getting data out of the country not internal comms

No doubt. Unless there's somebody friendly just across the border in Azerbaijan or Basrah or something, I don't see how they'd do it. Maybe point a dish and establish a point to point link, but you'd need to pre-arrange that.

在 HN 中查看
tgma 2026/04/16 13:27
我认为你今天的建议比以前更实际，因为 是 至少有一些人拥有某种访问权限。真正的问题在于准备，或者说缺乏准备。我周围的轶事是，他们希望（也许是一厢情愿）政权全面崩溃，互联网自由相对较快。
I think what you are suggesting is more practical today than before, since there are at least a few people who have some sort of access. The real catch is really the prep, or lack thereof. The anecdotes around me is they are hoping (perhaps wishfully) for a total regime collapse and internet freedom relatively soon.
在 HN 中查看
zenmac 2026/04/16 11:50
>NFP 的解决方案是添加冗余，原理上类似于称为 RAID（独立磁盘冗余阵列）的数据存储技术。我们不是发送每条数据一次，而是发送额外的信息，允许重建丢失或损坏的数据包。

老实说，如果这是他们的解决方案，那就有点令人失望。似乎一切都在试图将每个人归入工作证明场景中。如果我们有更多的力量/精力，那么我们将在安全、编码和审查规避等方面击败您。

>NFP’s solution was to add redundancy, similar in principle to a data-storage technique called RAID (redundant array of independent disks). Instead of sending each piece of data once, we send extra information that allows missing or corrupted packets to be reconstructed.

A bit disappointing TBH if that is their solution. Seems like everything is trying to pigeon hole everyone into the proof of work scenario. If we have more power/energy then we will beat you in everything security, coding and censorship circumvention.

在 HN 中查看
femto 2026/04/16 11:58
这是一个健全的解决方案。 他们正在描述具有可变码率的前向纠错。 降低码率会增加冗余量，从而允许在信噪比较低时恢复信号。 它是通信理论的标准部分，具有坚实的理论基础。 只要接收器不饱和，足够低的码率将克服几乎任何级别的干扰，但代价是降低数据率。
It's a sound solution. They are describing forward error correction with a variable code rate. Reducing the code rate increases the amount of redundancy, allowing the signal to be retrieved when the signal-to-noise ratio is lower. It's a standard part of communications theory and has a strong theoretical basis. A low enough code rate will overcome almost any level of jamming at the cost of reduced data rate, provided the receiver does not saturate.
在 HN 中查看
wiml 2026/04/16 12:00
我认为他们只是在做前向纠错（FEC）。不确定这与工作量证明有什么关系？
I think they're just doing forward error correction (FEC). Not sure what that has to do with proof-of-work?
在 HN 中查看
hgoel 2026/04/16 12:01
您建议采取什么其他方法来处理数据丢失？
What other method do you propose to deal with data loss?
在 HN 中查看
SanjayMehta 2026/04/16 12:22
镇压行动造成 3 万人死亡的说法值得怀疑。它源自一位医生、时尚博主、独立记者，名叫迪帕·帕伦特[1]，并且没有任何其他证据支持它。 （德累斯顿火灾轰炸需要大量弹药，死亡人数为 25,000 人）。[2]

话虽如此，鉴于伊朗神权政治的性质，他们完全有能力做出这样的行为。请记住，他们曾将同性恋者吊死在起重机上，[3] 并处决强奸受害者。[4]但两天内达到 30,000 个 [5] 是一个非同寻常的说法，需要更多的证据，当然不仅仅是追溯到一个来源的循环引用。

[1] https://thegrayzone.com/2026/02/01/guardian-iranian-death-to...

[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bombing_of_Dresden

[3] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahmoud_Asgari_and_Ayaz_Marhon...

[4] https://www.timesnownews.com/lifestyle/people/crime-against-...

[5] https://www.iranintl.com/en/202601255198

编辑：添加[5]

The claim of 30,000 dead in the crackdown is dubious. It originates from a doctor turned fashion blogger turned independent journalist called Deepa Parent[1] and doesn't have any other evidence to support it. (The Dresden fire bombing required tons of munitions and the toll was 25,000).[2]

Now having said that, given the nature of the Iranian theocracy, they are quite capable of such acts. Remember that they have hanged homosexuals from cranes,[3] and executed rape victims.[4] But 30,000 in two days [5] is an extraordinary claim which requires more evidence, certainly more than circular references tracing back to just one source.

[1] https://thegrayzone.com/2026/02/01/guardian-iranian-death-to...

[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bombing_of_Dresden

[3] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahmoud_Asgari_and_Ayaz_Marhon...

[4] https://www.timesnownews.com/lifestyle/people/crime-against-...

[5] https://www.iranintl.com/en/202601255198

Edit: added [5]

在 HN 中查看
at-fates-hands 2026/04/16 13:07
>> 但一天30,000

这个数字是在两天内给出的，而不是一天。

抗议活动在 200 多个城市举行，参与者超过 500 万人，我们知道他们发射了实弹，这是不可否认的，如果每个城市只有 200 人死亡，那么死亡人数就已经超过 4 万人了。是的，尚未验证，是的，没有并排显示 30000 具可识别尸体的视频。但这个数字实际上并不像人们想象的那样难以实现。

来自另一篇文章： 30,000 人的数字也远远超出了活动人士有条不紊地为死者指名所统计的数字。截至周六，美国人权活动家通讯社表示已确认 5,459 人死亡，并正在调查另外 17,031 人。

https://www.aol.com/articles/iran-protest-death-toll-could-1...

5,400 人的大规模屠杀在任何层面上都没有什么值得嘲笑的，即使是在两天之内。

>> But 30,000 in a day

The number was given over two days, not one.

The protests were in over 200 cities with 5 million+ participants, WE KNOW they fired live rounds this is undeniable if you only had 200 deaths per city you would reach a death toll north of 40k already. Yes it's not verified yes there isnt a video of exactly 30000 identifiable bodies side by side. But the number really isnt as unachievable as people make it out to be.

From another article: The 30,000 figure is also far beyond tallies being compiled by activists methodically assigning names to the dead. As of Saturday, the U.S.-based Human Rights Activists News Agency said it had confirmed 5,459 deaths and is investigating 17,031 more.

https://www.aol.com/articles/iran-protest-death-toll-could-1...

The mass murdering of 5,400 people is nothing to scoff at, on any level, even over two days.

在 HN 中查看

你是对的：两天。
You're right: two days.

mulmen 2026/04/16 13:19
> 这个数字是在两天内给出的，而不是一天。

是吗？ 我在链接的文章中找不到它。 与唯一的“30,000”事件相关的约 7,000 人死亡的消息来源称，这已经超过了第一个 五十 天。

> The number was given over two days, not one.

Was it? I can’t find that in the linked article. The source for the ~7,000 deaths linked near the only instance of “30,000” says it was over the first fifty days.

SanjayMehta 2026/04/16 13:39

https://www.iranintl.com/en/202601255198

It's not in this article but in others. I'm skeptical because the original source is that fashion blogger.

https://www.iranintl.com/en/202601255198

在 HN 中查看
mulmen 2026/04/16 13:43
目前尚不清楚这 30,000 个数字从何而来，因为该文章没有引用该数字的来源。 你在两篇（或多篇？）文章之间建立了未经证实的联系，这样你就可以援引并抹黑特定的记者。

现在我不确定你在说什么，但你似乎有一个议程。

It’s unclear where the 30,000 comes from because the article doesn’t cite a source for that number. You’re drawing an unsubstantiated connection between two (or more?) articles so you can invoke and then discredit a specific reporter.

At this point I’m not sure what you are even talking about but you seem to have an agenda.

在 HN 中查看
mulmen 2026/04/16 13:15
> 德累斯顿火灾轰炸需要大量弹药，死亡人数达 25,000 人

为了清楚起见 3,900 人 四次袭击和 1,500 架飞机投入了数吨。

我不知道如何将其转化为现代弹药，但我也不确定为什么战略轰炸死亡人数应该告诉我们有关伊朗抗议者死亡的任何信息。 伊朗并没有对其公民进行战略轰炸，而是在数百个地点使用小型武器。

链接的文章引用了一个来源 [1]，详细说明了他们实际声称的 7,000 人死亡的情况，并提到其他未提及的来源也声称这一点 可以 高达 30,000。 我没有看到任何这样的说法是一天之内发生的。

考虑到这些抗议活动的时间范围和广泛性，这似乎并不令人难以置信。

[1]： https://www.en-hrana.org/the-crimson-winter-a-50-day-record-...

> The Dresden fire bombing required tons of munitions and the toll was 25,000

For clarity 3,900 tons over four raids and 1,500 planes.

I’m not sure how to translate that into modern munitions but I’m also uncertain why a strategic bombing death toll should tell us anything about Iranian protester deaths. Iran isn’t doing strategic bombing of their citizens, they’re using small arms across hundreds of locations.

The linked article cites a source [1] with detailed methodology for their actual claim of 7,000 deaths and mentions that other unmentioned sources claim it could go as high as 30,000. I don’t see any claim of that being in a single day.

Given the timeframe and widespread nature of these protests it doesn’t seem implausible.

[1]: https://www.en-hrana.org/the-crimson-winter-a-50-day-record-...

在 HN 中查看
